title: Letting People Stay Who Surprised You
dek: You’ve been taught to brace for disappointment, to pre-judge others by their worst moments or the sharp edges that cut you deep. But what if the body remembers differently? What if the jaw unclenches long enough to allow surprise  -  a softening in the masseter and a release in the trapezius  -  inviting someone unexpected to remain in your life? This is not naivety; this is biology and fierce self-care.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Body Remembers, Even When You Try to Forget


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.173Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

It’s three in the morning, and the weight of the dark feels like a thick fog settling in the psoas muscle deep in your pelvis. You lie awake, jaw clenched, the masseter twitching with the memory of betrayal or disappointment. The body remembers the sting of surprise  -  when someone you thought you knew abruptly showed a face you never expected. This isn’t pathology; this is biology. The trapezius tightens around your neck, a silent sentinel guarding against further hurt.

You’ve been taught to anticipate the worst, to fold your fury inward like a secret herb tincture brewed in quiet shame. But the body’s wisdom is more nuanced. It can hold surprise without dissolving into chaos, holding space for the unexpected to live alongside the familiar. You smell the faint trace of mugwort burning in the kitchen - an old teacher’s recommendation to soothe restless nights and open the diaphragm to breath. Breathing into surprise means releasing the habitual grip, letting the levator scapulae soften, and acknowledging that people can shift beyond the roles you assigned them.

This is not about blind forgiveness or forgetting. It’s about allowing your nervous system to recalibrate, to stop bracing and start registering. The body knows when someone steps into your life with fresh intentions, even if your mind protests. Just as the earth shifts under the weight of rain, your muscles can relearn trust through the slow, deliberate practice of presence. 

## Unclenching the Jaw, Unfolding the Story

In the light of dawn, when the sky is a pale wash of grey and the air smells faintly of damp cedar, you might notice the tightness in your jaw - a clenched response to the surprise of someone’s kindness or change. The masseter muscle, often the repository of unspoken fury and guarded boundaries, has learned to contract so quickly that you barely notice it. But when you begin to trace your fingers along the jawline, feeling the tension dissolve like a bitter root steeping in hot water, you invite a different story to emerge.

Pema Chödrön, a teacher I return to when the body and heart feel tangled, reminds us that "the most fundamental aggression to ourselves, the most fundamental harm we can do to ourselves, is to remain ignorant by not having the courage and the respect to look at ourselves honestly and gently." This gentle honesty is what allows you to meet surprise without recoiling. It requires you to feel the levator scapulae loosen from its habitual tightness, to let the diaphragm drop and carry a full breath rather than a shallow gasp.

Letting people stay who surprise you means allowing the body to witness their unfolding without immediate judgment. It means noticing how the trapezius and masseter muscles twitch with old memories but choosing to stay present anyway. It’s an act of fierce self-respect, not weakness. You are not erasing the story of past wounds; you are expanding the narrative to include possibility. The body, with all its memories and muscle knots, is your clearest map through this terrain.

When you let people stay who surprise you, you’re saying yes to the complexity of human nature and to your own capacity for resilience. The body knows this deeply. It remembers the pulse of the levator scapulae releasing, the cool rush of blood to your cheeks as you soften your gaze, the subtle scent of lemon balm tea steeping nearby, grounding you in the here and now. You are learning to trust not just others, but the wisdom in your own trembling, unguarded muscles.

## The Lingering Weight in Your Trapezius

It’s just past midnight, the air outside thick with the scent of damp earth and the faint smoke from a neighbor’s fire. Inside, your body is still alert in the way it is when it’s startled but trying not to show it. Your trapezius muscles, those broad sheets of muscle running from the base of your skull down your neck and across your shoulders, tighten like a steel trap. You feel it there - right where the muscle fibers bunch near the cervical vertebrae - an involuntary brace against something unpredictable.

This is the physical echo of surprise, the body’s automatic armor. It’s not just a mental reaction. The trapezius is wired to respond to threats, real or imagined, and here it is, holding the memory of a moment when someone you thought you knew shifted into something unfamiliar and unruly. This muscle remembers the shock, the betrayal, even if your mind tries to shove it aside.

When you allow people to stay who surprise you - those unexpected eruptions in your carefully curated life - you’re inviting this tension to linger. You might notice it as a dull ache, a stiffness that won’t yield to stretching or massage. The fascia around the trapezius thickens, sticky with the residue of adrenaline and uncertainty. You could call it a scar. The body knows. It doesn’t forget these moments. It doesn’t forgive easily either, not without your permission.

I’m reminded here of Gabor Maté’s work on trauma and attachment. He speaks of how the body encodes emotional experience, how unmet needs and unexpected disruptions in trust become physical imprints. “The body keeps the score,” he says, echoing Bessel van der Kolk, but Maté goes further, emphasizing that these imprints are not signs of weakness or pathology - they are biology. They are survival mechanisms, the nervous system’s way of saying, “I’m on alert.”

So when someone surprises you - not with kindness or warmth, but with a fracture in your expectations - you don’t just hold that moment in your mind. You hold it in your trapezius, in the tiny motor units of the muscle fibers coiling shut to protect your neck and head. This is where fury begins to ferment, often unspoken, often unnamed. And telling yourself that you should have known better, that you should have seen the signs, only tightens the knot more.

## The Jaw Remembers What the Mouth Won’t Say

By the time the first light of dawn slips through your curtains, the masseter - the muscle responsible for jaw clenching - has been awake far longer than you. It contracts with silent insistence, a clenched hold that doesn’t relent even when you try to release it with soft words or distractions. The body remembers what the mouth won’t say, and your jaw is the archive of unvoiced fury and disbelief.

You might catch yourself grinding your teeth in the quiet moments, the subtle but relentless press of teeth against teeth that wears down enamel and frays patience. This is not just stress; it is the body’s refusal to let go of surprise, the refusal to normalize the rupture. The masseter muscle flexes like a clenched fist, a somatic shrine to boundaries felt but not spoken aloud.

I want to bring in the wisdom of herbal medicine here, which sees these tensions as calls for both release and resilience. A small cup of chrysanthemum flower tea, steeped long enough to bring out the cooling bitterness, can ease the heat lodged in your head and jaw. Chrysanthemum is not a sedative, but a gentle pacifier for the fire when fury simmers in the body’s tissues, especially around the head and neck. It reminds you: the body knows this is not pathology; it is biology asking for attention.

When you let people stay who surprise you, you are inviting a daily negotiation between your nervous system’s desire for safety and your heart’s stubborn refusal to exile them outright. This negotiation plays out in your diaphragm as well - the muscle that rides the rhythms of breath and emotion. Surprise tightens it, makes your breathing shallow, constricted like a bird caught in a cage. You might find yourself holding your breath at odd moments, the psoas and diaphragm caught in a silent standoff that leaves your torso feeling heavy and immobile.

The body insists on somatic honesty. It refuses to pretend all is well when something inside is still unsettled. So you breathe into the diaphragm, imagining it as a bellows that can expand and contract with the truth of your experience. You let the ribs rise slowly, deliberately, tracing the stretch of intercostal muscles and the soft expansion of the liver’s fascia underneath your right lung. This is where you hold your history of surprise and betrayal, and it is here you begin to soften.

You don’t have to let the surprise define the relationship, but you do have to honor the body’s response to it. The jaw remembers, the trapezius tightens, the diaphragm contracts - these are not flaws or failures. They are your ancestors speaking through muscle and fascia, telling you that you were not wrong to be startled, that your fury was necessary, that your boundary was real.

Letting people stay who surprise you means letting your body carry that truth without shame. It’s a somatic agreement that even when trust is fractured, the body’s memory is a sacred ledger. You might find, over time, that the masseter softens enough to allow words to come forth, that the trapezius unwinds into a cautious openness, that the diaphragm breathes with something like relief. This is not forgetting. This is not excusing. This is biology making space for complexity.

And you, in your muscle and marrow, begin to understand that the fury you were taught to hide is not ugly. It is the body’s way of saying, “I am alive. I am guarding what matters.” Let that be your guide as you live with the surprise, with the unexpected guests in your heart and body. The body knows. It always knows.

## When the Body Clenches Against Surprise

You might not notice it at first, but your body has a very particular way of telling you when you’ve been caught off guard by someone’s presence - especially those you thought you had neatly filed away as “done.” The jaw tightens, the masseter muscle knots like a clenched fist beneath your cheekbone. Maybe it’s the levator scapulae, that small muscle at the back of your neck, pulling up toward your ear in a stubborn shrug of disbelief. That tension isn’t just discomfort; it’s the body remembering an old story it wasn’t prepared to rewrite.

It’s late afternoon, the sharp light of a winter sun casting long shadows across your workspace where you sit with a cup of chrysanthemum tea, its faintly bitter floral scent curling in the air. You’re not just surprised by the person - they've reappeared in your life like a sudden cold draft through a cracked window - but your body is surprised, too, and that surprise is wrapped tightly in the fascia around your diaphragm, constricting your breath. This isn’t just a mental conflict; this is the body holding onto something it doesn’t quite know what to do with yet.

The fascinating thing is that this clenched, guarded response is not pathology. This is biology. The same muscles that react to physical threat react to emotional surprise. The body draws the line, saying, “Not yet, not so fast,” even when your mind might be willing to move forward. And here lies the paradox: letting people who surprise you stay means allowing your jaw to unclench first, letting the breath deepen, letting the planes of your face and the layers of your musculature soften rather than harden.

You might find, like I have, that this relaxation doesn’t happen in a flash. It’s a slow unwinding, a muscle-by-muscle negotiation. The trapezius, that broad sheet of muscle spanning your upper back, might hold a whisper of tension, a reminder of past vigilance. The psoas, deep within your pelvis, can tense reflexively, the body’s ancient way of bracing for fight or flight. But if you lean into the discomfort, if you lean into the surprise, you’ll find the body knows how to let go. You just have to invite it to remember.

## An Embodied Practice: Inviting the Jaw to Release

Find a quiet corner of your day, perhaps just before dawn when the world is still soft and the air smells faintly of damp earth and frost. Sit on the edge of your bed or a sturdy chair with your feet grounded firmly on the floor. Notice the weight of your pelvis connecting to the chair, the curve of your lower back, the alignment of your spine. There’s no rush here - only your breath and your body.

Begin by bringing your attention to your jaw. Without forcing anything, notice if it’s clenched or tight. Can you feel the masseter muscles pressing your teeth together like a clenched fist? Allow your tongue to rest gently against the roof of your mouth, just behind your front teeth. Now, with an exhale that lasts longer than your inhale, part your lips slightly. Feel the space that opens, the subtle release of tension beneath your cheekbones.

As you soften the jaw, take your awareness down to your neck. The levator scapulae often tenses in response to emotional surprise - imagine it as a small knot that can slowly unravel with kindness. Tilt your head gently to one side, ear moving toward the shoulder, and hold for a few breaths. Don’t stretch to pain, stretch to ease. Then return to center and repeat on the other side.

Now place one hand softly on your belly, just below the ribcage, feeling the diaphragm move with each breath. Inhale gently through your nose, allowing your belly to expand against your hand. Exhale slowly through your mouth, imagining the tension around your diaphragm melting away like frost in morning sun. This breath is medicine; it tells the body that you are safe enough to relax, even in the face of surprise.

Throughout this practice, you might smell the faint aroma of herbs steeping nearby - perhaps a cup of warm mugwort or a tincture of skullcap waiting to support your nervous system. These plants are not magic; they are precise, reliable medicine you call upon to soften the body’s edges, to help the fascia and muscles remember how to yield.

Once you feel your jaw and neck soften and your breath deepen, sit for a moment longer, allowing the body’s memory to shift. This is not about erasing the surprise or pretending it didn’t rattle you. This is about letting the body acknowledge its own wisdom. The jaw remembers the old story, yes, but it also remembers how to unclench. You are inviting the body to hold space for this unexpected presence without folding into old patterns of defense.

Letting people who surprise you stay is a practice of radical hospitality - not just toward others, but toward the body that carries all your histories. It’s an unflinching acceptance of your own biology, your own muscle memory, and the possibility that the story you thought was finished might still have chapters left to write.

## Section 7: Letting People Stay Who Surprised You

You know that moment when your jaw clenches tightly, the masseter muscle knotting like a fist behind your cheekbones, and your breath hitches somewhere low in the diaphragm? That’s the body remembering - holding onto every flicker of surprise, every betray, every unexpected kindness that unsettles your mental script. When someone surprises you, especially in a way that contradicts your internal warning system, it’s like a sudden cold snap in early November, chilling the fascia beneath your skin and demanding attention.

Letting those people stay isn’t about rewriting the past or ignoring the body’s signals. It’s about acknowledging that the body knows how to hold complexity. The trapezius might still tighten when they walk into the room, a reflexive brace against the unknown, but you can choose to breathe into that tension. You can choose to let the levator scapulae soften, allowing the shoulders to drop away from your ears. This is not pathology; this is biology. The body is simply doing its job, protecting you from what it perceives as a threat.

But when you decide to let them stay, you are telling your nervous system a new story. You are saying, “I see the surprise, I feel the tension, and I am choosing to remain open.” It’s a slow, deliberate unwinding of the psoas, a conscious release of the grip you’ve held on your own expectations. You might find yourself reaching for a cup of lemon balm tea, its gentle, lemony scent a balm for the frayed edges of your nerves, reminding you that you have the tools to soothe yourself even when the world is unpredictable.

This is the work of somatic honesty. It’s not about forcing a smile or pretending the surprise didn’t rattle you. It’s about letting the body speak its truth - the clenched jaw, the shallow breath, the tight shoulders - and then gently, persistently, inviting it to soften. It’s about recognizing that the fury you were taught to hide is not ugly; it’s a vital part of your survival. And when you let people stay who surprise you, you are honoring that survival while also making space for something new to grow.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Q:** How do I know if my body is reacting to a genuine threat or just an unexpected surprise?
**A:** The body knows the difference, even if your mind is confused. A genuine threat often feels like a sharp, immediate contraction - a sudden tightening of the psoas, a racing heart, a feeling of cold dread in the pit of your stomach. An unexpected surprise, on the other hand, might feel more like a startling jolt followed by a lingering tension in the masseter or trapezius. It’s a brace against the unknown rather than a full-blown panic. Pay attention to how quickly the tension dissipates when you take a slow, deep breath. If the body begins to soften, it’s likely a surprise rather than a threat.

**Q:** What if I can’t stop clenching my jaw when I’m around someone who surprised me?
**A:** This is not pathology; this is biology. Your masseter muscle is simply doing what it was designed to do - protecting you from perceived danger. Instead of fighting the clench, try to bring awareness to it. Notice the exact moment your jaw tightens. Is it when they speak? When they move a certain way? Once you identify the trigger, you can begin to gently invite the jaw to release. You might also find support in herbal allies like skullcap or chamomile, which can help soothe the nervous system and ease muscular tension.

**Q:** Is it okay to feel angry when someone surprises me, even if the surprise is positive?
**A:** Absolutely. The body remembers every time it was caught off guard, and it doesn’t always distinguish between a positive surprise and a negative one. The sudden shift in expectations can trigger a defensive response, and anger is often the body’s first line of defense. Your fury is not ugly; it’s a sign that your boundaries are intact. Allow yourself to feel the anger, to notice where it lives in your body - perhaps a heat in the chest or a tightness in the throat - and then let it move through you.

**Q:** How can I practice letting people stay when my instinct is to push them away?
**A:** Start small. You don’t have to invite them into your inner sanctum right away. Begin by simply noticing your body’s reaction when they are near. Feel the tension in your levator scapulae, the shallow breath in your diaphragm. Acknowledge the instinct to push them away, and then take one slow, deliberate breath. Remind yourself that you are safe, that you have the tools to protect yourself if necessary. Letting them stay is a practice of expanding your capacity for discomfort, of allowing the body to hold both the surprise and the safety simultaneously.

## The Somatic Echo of Surprise

When you finally decide to let someone stay who has surprised you, the body doesn’t just instantly relax. It’s not a light switch you can flip. Instead, it’s a slow, deliberate thawing, like ice melting off the eaves in late winter. The masseter muscle, which has been holding your jaw in a vice grip for days, might finally begin to soften, but it will do so in increments. You’ll notice it first in the quiet moments - perhaps when you’re standing at the kitchen sink, the smell of damp earth and decaying leaves drifting in through the open window, and you realize your teeth aren’t clamped together anymore.

This is the somatic echo of surprise. It lingers in the fascia, in the connective tissue that wraps around your muscles like a second skin. The trapezius might still hold a knot of tension, a small, hard reminder of the moment your expectations were shattered. But as you continue to breathe into that space, as you continue to invite the body to soften, the knot will begin to unravel. It’s a process of renegotiating your relationship with the unexpected, of teaching your nervous system that surprise doesn’t always equal danger.

You might find yourself drawn to warming herbs during this time - ginger, cinnamon, perhaps a touch of cayenne. These plants bring heat to the body, encouraging circulation and helping to flush out the stagnant energy that often accompanies a shock to the system. They remind the body that it is alive, that it is capable of movement and change. And as the heat spreads through your limbs, you might feel a subtle shift in your psoas, a releasing of the deep, primal grip that has been holding you in a state of readiness.

Letting people stay who surprise you is an act of profound courage. It requires you to sit with the discomfort of the unknown, to trust the wisdom of your own body even when your mind is screaming at you to run. It’s a practice of somatic honesty, of acknowledging the fury and the fear without letting them dictate your actions. And in the end, it’s a testament to the resilience of the human spirit - the ability to hold space for the unexpected, to allow the body to soften, and to welcome the surprise with open arms.

## The Biology of Staying

It’s important to remember that this process - this slow unwinding of the jaw, this deliberate softening of the trapezius - is not a sign of weakness. It is the biology of staying. When you choose to let someone stay who has surprised you, you are actively rewiring your nervous system. You are teaching your body that it is safe to experience the unexpected, that it doesn’t have to immediately default to a state of high alert.

This rewiring takes time, and it requires patience. You might find that the tension returns, that the masseter clenches again at the slightest provocation. This is normal. The body remembers, and it will always try to protect you. But each time you notice the tension, each time you consciously choose to breathe into it and invite it to release, you are strengthening the new neural pathways. You are building a somatic resilience that will serve you well in all areas of your life.

And as you continue this practice, you might begin to notice a shift in how you experience surprise. Instead of a sudden, jarring shock, it might become a gentle ripple, a momentary pause before the body settles back into a state of equilibrium. You might find that you are more open to the unexpected, more willing to embrace the complexity of human nature. You might even find that you are able to welcome the surprise with a sense of curiosity, rather than fear.

This is the ultimate gift of letting people stay who surprise you. It’s not just about them; it’s about you. It’s about expanding your capacity for life, for all its messy, unpredictable, beautiful complexity. It’s about trusting the wisdom of your own body, and knowing that whatever comes your way, you have the tools to handle it. The body knows. It always knows.
